triage_reasoning: C3 is a secreted plasma protein synthesized primarily by the liver and macrophages. It circulates in blood as a soluble alpha-beta disulfide-linked heterodimer (~185 kDa). It has no transmembrane domain, no GPI anchor, and no known stable membrane partner that would co-traffic it to the cell surface. Checking each contextual bucket: (1) cell_state_induced — C3 deposits covalently on opsonized targets via its thioester-mediated attachment, but this is matrix/surface deposition on pathogens/debris, not PM display of the C3 protein body accessible from outside a host cell. (2) tissue_restricted_surface — no evidence of lineage-specific PM display. (3) lysosomal_exocytosis — not documented. (4) dual_localization — no evidence of a stable PM pool; covalent deposition on cells is on target cells/pathogens, not on C3-secreting cells. (5) stable_surface_attachment — opsonization deposits C3b/iC3b on target surfaces, but this reflects extracellular covalent attachment to glycoproteins/carbohydrates on those target cells, not the C3-producing cell's own PM. No antibody/ADC/CAR-T programs target C3 on a host cell surface. Anti-C3 therapeutics (e.g., pegcetacoplan) neutralize the soluble circulating form. Verdict: secreted_only.

executive_summary.one_paragraph: C3 is state-dependently surface-accessible as a secreted complement glycoprotein — no transmembrane domain, with surface presence achieved exclusively via thioester-mediated covalent deposition of activated C3b/iC3b fragments onto target cell surfaces. Direct surface evidence is single-method: live-cell flow cytometry detecting C3 deposition on infected cells (a1_evi_23) and a functional assay showing CR2-fH reducing C3b deposits on RPE cell membranes (a1_evi_42); IHC in human tauopathy brain shows neuronal C3b/iC3b deposition (a1_evi_22). Surface presence is strictly state-gated, requiring complement activation triggers — viral infection, thromboinflammation, oncogenic transformation, or neurodegenerative disease — and is absent in normal resting tissue (a2_evi_12, a2_evi_13). A dominant free-soluble circulating pool is the principal antibody-decoy risk (a1_evi_01, a1_evi_03); moderate conformational epitope masking arises from activation-state-dependent neoepitope exposure (a1_evi_17, a1_evi_18).
executive_summary.accessibility_context_summary: Surface-accessible only in disease states (complement activation, viral infection, tauopathy, thromboinflammation, tumor), where activated C3b/iC3b fragments deposit covalently onto target cell surfaces; the dominant C3 pool is freely circulating/secreted and not surface-anchored in normal resting tissue.
hgnc_gene_groups: C3 and PZP like, alpha-2-macroglobulin domain containing

filters.expression_level_rationale: C3 is the most abundant complement component in circulation, synthesized at high levels by hepatocytes (a2_evi_01); also expressed at moderate levels in immune cells, epithelial cells, and multiple non-hepatic cell types (a2_evi_04, a2_evi_05).
filters.expression_breadth_rationale: C3 is expressed across liver, immune cells (T cells, monocytes, macrophages, eosinophils), retinal cells (RPE, Müller glia, photoreceptors), epithelial cells, fibroblasts, astrocytes, mast cells, and adipose macrophages (a2_evi_01, a2_evi_04, a2_evi_05, a2_evi_08, a2_evi_30, a2_evi_31).
filters.surface_specificity_rationale: C3 is a secreted glycoprotein with no TM domain; the dominant pool is freely circulating/secreted or intracellular (complosome), with surface presence only via state-dependent covalent deposition of activated fragments (a1_evi_01, a1_evi_03, a2_evi_28).
filters.has_known_ligand_rationale: C3b binds complement receptors CR1 (CD35), CR2 (CD21), CR3 (CD11b/CD18), and CR4 (CD11c/CD18) as validated endogenous binding partners mediating opsonisation and immune cell engagement (a2_evi_28).
filters.low_endogenous_expression_rationale: C3 is the most abundant complement component in circulation, synthesized at high levels by hepatocytes (a2_evi_01); also expressed at moderate levels in immune cells, epithelial cells, and multiple non-hepatic cell types (a2_evi_04, a2_evi_05).
filters.overexpression_surface_localization_obse: No method observation pairs an overexpression/mixed expression system with a direct or supportive surface-accessibility readout.

surface_evidence.grade_rationale: The methods builder identified two direct_surface_accessibility rows: live_cell_flow (a1_evi_23, a1_evi_24) and a functional_surface_assay (a1_evi_42). All three are human-anchored. However, critical context: (1) a1_evi_23 shows surface C3 deposition on virus-infected cells by live flow (no permeabilization), single source, infection-state dependent (a1_evi_23); (2) a1_evi_24 shows live flow iC3b on serum-treated bacteria surfaces — the 'cell surface' here is a non-host bacterial target, not a human host cell PM (a1_evi_24); (3) a1_evi_42 shows CR2-fH therapeutically reducing C3b deposition on RPE cell membranes in a live-cell functional assay (a1_evi_42). Supporting IHC membranous from human tauopathy neurons (a1_evi_22) and IF localization on human photoreceptors (a1_evi_38) add indirect support. Critically, what is detected in all surface-positive claims is deposited C3b/iC3b fragments (or activation-state-deposited C3), not a stably PM-resident C3 protein expressed by the cell itself. The triage verdict is 'secreted_only' and most claims confirm C3 is secreted/intracellular with surface presence only via thioester-mediated covalent deposition on target cells in specific states. Given ≥1 direct method row with human species (a1_evi_23, a1_evi_42), grade is direct_single_method with low confidence — the surface presence is state/deposition-conditional on non-producing target cells.

claim_stances.note: Structural classification: secreted AMCOM thioester protein, no TM domain — describes baseline biology, not a refutation of surface deposition
Thioester/furin cleavage site — mechanism underlying covalent surface attachment; not surface-accessibility evidence per se
Canonical description: secreted/intracellular glycoprotein, no TM domain — baseline biology, not incompatible with surface deposition
C3b thioester-mediated covalent surface attachment — review-level assertion, fragment not intact C3
Surface-bound C3b forms AP proconvertase — review-level; fragment-level surface presence
Fixed/permeabilized flow cytometry — detects intracellular C3 in VECs; not surface-accessibility evidence
Fixed/permeabilized flow — quantifies C3-positive VECs; intracellular pool, no surface localization
Fixed/permeabilized flow — C3b-positive VECs baseline/infection; not surface-specific
Antibody clone detail for VEC flow experiments — methodological annotation
Gating strategy for VEC flow — methodological; cells are live but antibody applied after fixation/perm per study design
Flow synaptometry on mouse synaptosome membranes detects C3b surface deposition on synaptic particles — non-human, particle not live cell PM
ECM shielding of C3-tagging — contextual biology, weak refutation; mechanistic not direct
Exogenous C3/C3b added to platelets in binding assay — soluble C3 engaging platelet surface, not C3 anchored on C3-producing cell; excluded as ligand engagement
Native C3 binding to activated platelets (exogenous ligand assay) — soluble C3 engaging platelet PM; excluded as ligand engagement
Annexin V competition for C3 binding on platelets — tests soluble C3 as ligand, not anchored surface form; excluded as ligand engagement
Review assertion: C3(H2O) bound on activated platelets/ECs in thromboinflammation — surface association of C3 fragment under pathological state
Antibody clones for sandwich ELISA detection of C3 conformational states — methodological detail
mAb validation of C3 conformational change on hydrolysis — methodological
iPSC-RPE WB shows C3a/C3(H2O)/iC3b secreted apically; dominant secreted not membrane-anchored — bulk protein without surface localization
IF/WB iPSC-RPE: apical + intracellular C3 β-chain; permeabilization status unspecified — does not distinguish surface from intracellular
Permeabilized IF for intracellular C3 processing in iPSC-RPE — methodological, intracellular
IHC on post-mortem human tauopathy brain: C3b/iC3b deposition on neurons — IHC membranous, human tissue, tauopathy state
Live-cell surface deposition of C3 on infected cells by flow (no permeabilization) — direct surface accessibility, single source, infection state
Live flow on serum-treated bacteria: iC3b dominant surface fragment — direct surface, non-host cell target
Rat anti-C3/C3b antibody clone detail — methodological annotation, rat species
IHC: C3 in protein casts and proximal tubules in nephrotic mouse kidney — tissue localization, surface vs intracellular not distinguished
C3-KO mouse model — genetic control validating specificity; methodological support
iC3b on nanoparticle surface driving macrophage uptake — nanoparticle/EV-like surface, not host cell PM; excluded as non-cell-PM context
Soft-corona masking of C3 on nanoparticle surface — nanoparticle system, not host cell PM
Crystal structure compstatin–C3c — structural biology of soluble C3c, not cell-surface accessibility
Cryo-EM of C3bB–Cp60 complex — structural; soluble C3b complex, not cell-surface evidence
Compstatins bind C3 ECD — therapeutic engagement of soluble/deposited C3; not anchored surface evidence
Cryo-EM C3 substrate binding to C4b convertase — structural; soluble C3 as substrate in convertase complex
Anti-C3 nanobody hC3Nb3 in therapeutic construct — engages soluble/deposited C3; methodological
Review: intracellular C3 in hepatocyte autophagy/ER/lipid — describes intracellular pool; consistent with secreted/intracellular not PM
Flow cytometry for intracellular C3 in HBV immune cells — permeabilized, intracellular; no surface component
RT-qPCR: C3 mRNA decreased in CHB liver biopsy — RNA only, no surface information
IF: C3 and CFH localized on human photoreceptors — fixed IF, perm status unspecified; surface-localized staining in specialized neurons
IF: C3 deposition on Panc02 cancer cell surface post-treatment — mouse cell line, perm status unspecified
FHR51-9FH1-5 binding to surface-immobilized C3/C3b in ELISA — plate-immobilized C3 as ligand, not cell-surface C3
Intracellular AP activation in FRCs after SFTSV infection — intracellular C3a generation; contextualizes non-PM C3 pools
CR2-fH reduces C3b deposition on RPE cell membranes (ARPE-19, live cells, non-permeabilized) — direct functional surface assay; C3b accessible on RPE PM
Efb-C binds C3b TED — structural/biochemical engagement of C3b fragment; not cell-surface accessibility assay
WB: C3 protein in whole placental tissue extract — bulk protein, no surface fractionation

antibodies.name: anti-C3b/iC3b/C3d
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: Recognizes human C3b, iC3b, C3dg and intact C3; vendor states antibody stains extracellular domain of C3b/iC3b/C3d.

antibodies.name: anti-C3
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: Anti-total-C3 antibody used for C3 detection in same panel; clone and vendor not specified in ledger.

expression_observations.context: Uninfected primary human vaginal epithelial cells (VECs): ~49.8% C3-positive by permeabilized flow cytometry
Candida albicans-infected VECs: C3-positive fraction rises to ~63%; C3b-positive fraction rises from 8.5% to 20–30% post-infection

antibodies.name: anti-C3
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: Epitope-specific antibodies used per methods description; specific clone and vendor not stated in ledger.

expression_observations.context: Highly mature (HM) iPSC-RPE: apical localization of C3 protein with intracellular C3 β-chain retention
Less mature (LM) iPSC-RPE and ARPE-19: C3 fragments detected intracellularly

antibodies.name: anti-C3b/iC3b
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: Detects C3b/iC3b activation neoepitopes; no clone or vendor stated in ledger for this study.

expression_observations.context: Post-mortem human tauopathy brain: C3b/iC3b deposition particularly prominent on neurons vs controls

antibodies.name: anti-C3
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: Surface C3 deposition measured on non-permeabilized intact infected cells; antibody clone/vendor not specified in ledger.

expression_observations.context: Persistently infected (PI) cells: surface C3 deposition greatly reduced vs acutely infected (AI) cells; non-permeabilized live-cell flow readout

antibodies.name: anti-iC3b
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: Assay confirmed only iC3b (not C3b) present on bacterial surface due to rapid C3b cleavage; clone/vendor not stated in ledger.

expression_observations.context: Human-serum-treated Haemophilus bacteria: iC3b is the dominant surface-deposited C3 fragment; C3b rapidly cleaved on serum-resistant isolates

antibodies.name: anti-C3
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: Specificity validated by C3-knockout (C3−/−) mouse controls used in the same study; clone/vendor not specified in ledger.

expression_observations.context: Nephrotic mouse kidney: positive C3 staining in protein casts and within proximal tubule epithelial cells by IHC
Immune cells from chronic HBV-infected patients: intracellular C3 levels measured by permeabilized flow cytometry

antibodies.name: anti-C3
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: Clone and vendor not stated; paper describes C3 and CFH localization on human photoreceptors.

expression_observations.context: Human retina: C3 localized on photoreceptors by immunostaining, supporting non-canonical surface localization in a specialized sensory neuron

antibodies.name: anti-C3
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: Clone and vendor not stated; C3 deposition detected on cancer cell surface after complement-activating treatment.

expression_observations.context: Panc02 murine pancreatic cancer cells: C3 deposition on cell surface evidenced by immunostaining after complement activation treatment
Whole placental tissue extracts from pre-eclampsia and gestational diabetes pregnancies: C3 protein detected by Western blot without subcellular fractionation
ARPE-19 / iPSC-RPE cells: CR2-fH complement inhibitor targeting surface-deposited C3b on RPE cell membranes significantly reduced C3b deposition and downstream complement activation

non_surface_expression.context: Vaginal epithelial cells (VECs) — baseline and Candida albicans infection; intracellular C3, C3a, C3b by permeabilized flow cytometry
iPSC-derived retinal pigment epithelium (iPSC-RPE) — apical localization and intracellular C3 β-chain; secreted C3a/C3(H2O)/iC3b by WB and IF
Immune cells of chronic HBV-infected patients — intracellular C3 by permeabilized flow cytometry
Liver biopsy tissue from chronic hepatitis B patients — C3 mRNA by RT-qPCR; decreased vs uninfected
Mouse nephrotic kidney — proximal tubule epithelial cells and protein casts; C3 protein by IHC
Human placenta (pre-eclampsia / gestational diabetes) — whole tissue extract C3 protein by Western blot

contradicting_evidence.claim: C3 is a secreted glycoprotein with no transmembrane domain, present both in circulation and intracellularly in hepatocytes. A significant intracellular pool of C3 in hepatocytes serves functions in autophagy, ER function, and lipid homeostasis, consistent with a secreted/intracellular rather than plasma-membrane-anchored classification.
contradicting_evidence.likely_explanation: C3 lacks a transmembrane domain and is a classical secreted complement protein. Intracellular C3 in hepatocytes represents a biosynthetic/pre-secretory pool or a distinct intracellular complement system, not surface-anchored protein. Any apparent surface detection likely reflects covalent deposition of activated C3 fragments (C3b, iC3b) on cell surfaces rather than constitutive transmembrane expression.

contradicting_evidence.claim: In iPSC-derived retinal pigment epithelium cells, C3 and its fragments (C3a, C3(H2O), iC3b) are predominantly secreted apically and basally in mature cells, while less mature cells retain C3 fragments intracellularly. This partitioning between secretion and intracellular retention demonstrates that C3 is not a stable plasma-membrane protein in RPE.
contradicting_evidence.likely_explanation: RPE cells are known to secrete complement components as part of their homeostatic function. Intracellular retention in less mature cells likely reflects incomplete processing through the secretory pathway rather than a distinct surface-expression compartment. The dominant fate of C3 in RPE is secretion, not membrane anchoring.

contradicting_evidence.claim: Intracellular complement activation in spleen stromal fibroblastic reticular cells (FRCs) generates C3a via the alternative pathway following SFTSV infection, demonstrating that C3 can be activated entirely within the intracellular compartment in non-hepatic cell types, further reinforcing that C3 biology is not restricted to the plasma membrane surface.
contradicting_evidence.likely_explanation: Intracellular complement activation is a recognized parallel system to classical extracellular complement. C3 synthesized within FRCs undergoes intracellular processing without obligate surface trafficking. This does not preclude transient surface deposition of activated fragments, but underscores that C3 is not a bona fide surface-resident protein.

contradicting_evidence.claim: ECM components (brevican, aggrecan) in mouse cerebellum can shield synaptic surfaces from C3b tagging, and protein corona components on nanoparticles can mask HC-bound C3 fragments from complement receptor recognition. These epitope-masking phenomena indicate that even when C3 fragments are deposited on surfaces, their accessibility to receptors can be substantially reduced by surrounding molecular environments.
contradicting_evidence.likely_explanation: These masking effects are context-dependent and do not negate C3 surface deposition per se; rather, they modulate functional accessibility of deposited C3 fragments. For target-discovery purposes, the relevant question is whether C3 fragments are present on cell surfaces, which these findings do not directly contradict — they only show that detection or receptor engagement can be hindered in specific microenvironments.

excluded_as_ligand_engagement.reason: Exogenous C3/C3b/C3(H2O) added to activated platelets in binding assay — soluble C3 acting as ligand engaging platelet PM phosphatidylserine; not C3 anchored on C3-producing cell surface.
Native C3 binding to activated platelets — soluble circulating C3 engaging platelet phospholipid-scrambled membranes; C3 is the soluble ligand, not the surface-anchored entity.

Annexin V competition assay testing soluble C3 binding to platelets, PMN, and ECs via phosphatidylserine — C3 is soluble ligand engaging PS receptor on target cells, not anchored at C3-producing cell surface.
iC3b on nanoparticle hard corona drives macrophage uptake via complement receptors — C3 fragment is on nanoparticle surface engaging CR on macrophages; not host cell PM surface accessibility.
FHR51-9FH1-5 binding to plate-immobilized C3/C3b/iC3b/C3d in ELISA — C3 is plate-immobilized antigen acting as ligand; not cell-surface accessibility evidence.

expression.disease_label: luminal breast cancer lymph node metastasis

dual_localization.condition: intracellular complosome pool in hepatocytes, immune cells, and epithelial cells
C3(H2O) or C3b covalently bound to activated platelets and endothelial cells in thromboinflammatory disorders
intracellular roles in ER function in hepatocytes

anatomical_accessibility.rationale: Immunostaining of high-maturation iPSC-RPE directly resolved C3 to the apical membrane face. In the subretinal space, the apical RPE surface faces the photoreceptors/subretinal space rather than the choroidal blood supply, limiting access for systemically delivered binders.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: liver biopsy tissue from uninfected individuals
accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: liver biopsy tissue from chronic hepatitis B (CHB) patients
accessibility_modulation.change: C3 mRNA expression is profoundly decreased in liver biopsy of CHB patients compared to uninfected individuals, indicating disease-state suppression of hepatic C3 production.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Reduced hepatic C3 synthesis lowers serum C3 availability; less circulating C3 means reduced opsonisation and surface deposition of C3b on targets in CHB patients.

accessibility_modulation.change: In hepatocytes, C3 occupies both an intracellular compartment (autophagy, ER, lipid metabolism roles) and a secreted extracellular pool; the intracellular fraction is not surface-accessible.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: A substantial intracellular C3 pool in hepatocytes is not accessible to extracellular binders; only the secreted fraction becomes surface-opsonising C3b after complement activation.

accessibility_modulation.change: SFTSV infection significantly induces intracellular C3 production in splenic FRCs via a type I IFN-independent mechanism, generating active C3a via the alternative pathway intracellularly.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Induced intracellular C3/C3a in FRCs is not surface-exposed; this represents a compartmentalised complosome response not accessible to extracellular binders during viral infection.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: resting human Müller glia (MIO-M1 / primary retinal Müller cells)
accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: pro-inflammatory cytokine-stimulated Müller glia (IFNγ, TNFα, IL-4, IL-6, IL-10, VEGF, TGFβ)
accessibility_modulation.change: Cytokine stimulation of Müller cells induces secretion of complement components including C3, shifting C3 from an intracellular/non-secreted state to an extracellular secreted pool.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Secreted C3 from activated Müller cells becomes available for complement activation in the retinal extracellular space, increasing local C3b deposition and opsonisation potential.

accessibility_modulation.change: High-maturation iPSC-RPE secretes increased C3a, C3(H2O), and iC3b extracellularly and shows apical C3 localisation, whereas low-maturation RPE retains C3 fragments intracellularly with no surface/secreted complement.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Maturation-gated secretion and apical polarisation of C3 in high-maturation RPE means extracellular C3 (accessible to binders) is a maturation-dependent feature; immature RPE presents no accessible C3.

accessibility_modulation.change: In high-maturation polarised RPE, C3 localises apically; in low-maturation non-polarised RPE, C3 remains intracellular. Apical polarisation gates surface-accessible C3.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Apical C3 in mature polarised RPE is accessible only from the apical (subretinal) side; basolateral binders would not reach this C3 pool.

accessibility_modulation.change: C3 protein and C3a are abundantly present in Hh-type medulloblastoma tumor sections but undetectable in adjacent normal brain tissue by IHC, establishing tumor-restricted C3 accumulation.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: C3 accumulation is restricted to the tumor microenvironment; a C3-targeting binder would selectively engage tumor tissue over normal CNS, potentially enabling tumor-selective targeting.

accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: tauopathy brain tissue (Pick's disease, CBD, GGT)
accessibility_modulation.change: C3b/iC3b deposition is elevated in all tauopathy subtypes vs controls by IHC, particularly on neuronal surfaces, demonstrating disease-state-induced C3 fragment deposition on neuronal cell surfaces.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Increased C3b/iC3b on neuronal surfaces in tauopathy creates accessible complement opsonins on neurons; these could be engaged by complement-receptor-based therapeutics or imaging agents.

accessibility_modulation.change: COVID-19 lung tissue shows extensive deposition of C3, C3b/iC3b/C3d compared to normal lung, indicating hyper-activation of complement with massive C3 fragment deposition in alveolar compartments.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Heavy C3/C3b surface deposition in COVID-19 lung creates abundant extracellular complement opsonins accessible to binders; this may drive immunopathology via complement receptor engagement.

accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: kidney glomeruli in C3 glomerulopathy patients
accessibility_modulation.change: C3 glomerulopathy is characterised by abnormal glomerular C3 deposition due to complement alternative pathway dysregulation, accumulating C3 fragments in glomerular structures.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Glomerular C3 deposits are anatomically accessible within the kidney filtration unit; this represents a disease-state-specific surface-associated C3 pool targetable in nephrology.

accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: injured renal tubules in rhabdomyolysis-associated AKI (RIAKI)
accessibility_modulation.change: C3 staining is detected in injured renal tubules in RIAKI patients, often surrounded by C5aR1-expressing myeloid cells, indicating disease-state-induced C3 deposition on tubular epithelial surfaces.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: C3 present on injured tubular surfaces is accessible to complement receptors on infiltrating myeloid cells; represents a disease-induced accessible C3 pool in acute kidney injury.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: primary luminal breast cancer tumor tissue
accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: lymph node metastases of luminal breast cancer
accessibility_modulation.change: C3 protein expression is significantly less frequent in lymph node metastases compared to matched primary tumors by IHC; complement expression declines during lymphatic spread.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Lower C3 in metastatic lesions compared to primary tumor means C3-dependent opsonisation or complement activation is reduced at metastatic sites, potentially limiting immune surveillance there.

accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: placental tissue from preeclampsia (PE) or recurrent pregnancy loss (RPL) patients
accessibility_modulation.change: C3 and C5 levels are elevated in placental tissue of PE and RPL patients compared to normal pregnancy, suggesting complement overactivation in these obstetric disease states.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Elevated C3 in diseased placental tissue increases local complement deposition and C3b surface opsonisation potential, which may contribute to placental injury.

accessibility_modulation.change: C3a-positive VEC fraction increases significantly from 5.4% at baseline to 21.7% (colonising) and 27.7% (VVC) after Candida infection, indicating infection-induced intracellular C3 activation in VECs.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Infection-driven C3 cleavage within VECs generates intracellular C3a; this intracellular pool is not surface-accessible to extracellular binders but signals intracellular complement activation.

accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: aged humans and mice (plasma and visceral adipose tissue)
accessibility_modulation.change: Circulating C3a is elevated during aging; an age-associated macrophage subset expanding in visceral adipose tissue is identified as the predominant extrahepatic C3 source. Caloric restriction reduces the C3a/C3 ratio.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Elevated systemic C3a in aging reflects increased C3 cleavage and complement activation; a higher circulating C3a/C3b pool means increased complement opsonisation activity in aged individuals.

accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: activated platelets and endothelial cells in thromboinflammatory disorders
accessibility_modulation.change: C3(H2O) exists in a cell-bound form on activated platelets and endothelial cells in thromboinflammatory disorders; native C3 binds most efficiently to phospholipid-scrambled (activated) platelet surfaces.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Disease- and activation-state-dependent surface binding of C3/C3(H2O) on platelets and endothelial cells creates an accessible C3 pool on these cell surfaces, enabling convertase formation and further complement amplification.

accessibility_modulation.change: C3b staining is augmented in kidney and spleen tissue from Hmox1-/- rats due to reduced complement regulatory proteins DAF and Crry, leading to increased C3b deposition in these tissues.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Increased C3b surface deposition in HO-1-deficient tissues reflects loss of complement regulation; more surface-bound C3b is accessible to complement receptors and downstream effectors.

accessibility_modulation.change: Multi-omics analysis shows C3 is upregulated as an inflammatory mediator in IL-33 + TNF-α stimulated eosinophils alongside surface markers CD22, CD4, and ICAM-1.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Cytokine-induced C3 upregulation in eosinophils may increase local complement production and secretion in inflammatory settings, enhancing C3-dependent opsonisation in the tissue microenvironment.

biological_context.grade_rationale: All four A2 axes are well-populated from multiple independent sources: expression mapped across ≥10 cell/tissue types (liver, spleen, retina, brain, lung, kidney, skin, adipose, placenta, epithelial/immune cells); subcellular localization pinned (intracellular complosome vs. secreted); anatomical accessibility detailed (opsonization, platelet/endothelial surface binding); modulation documented across disease states, aging, cytokine stimulation, and maturation state. Picture is coherent — context-dependent variation is biologically consistent.

surface_bind.attribution: © Balbi et al., Correia lab — EPFL / Inria / Novo Nordisk

co_receptor_requirements.rationale: C3 is a secreted glycoprotein with no transmembrane domain (a1_evi_01, a1_evi_03). Its surface association is achieved post-activation via covalent thioester transacylation of C3b onto target surfaces (a2_evi_28), not via a co-receptor or trafficking partner. No chaperone or obligate escort is documented in the ledger.

restricted_subdomain.rationale: In polarized iPSC-RPE cells, C3 shows apical localization by immunostaining (a1_evi_20, a2_evi_10). However, C3b deposits broadly on pathogen/cell surfaces without documented apical restriction in most cell types (a1_evi_22, a2_evi_13, a2_evi_15). The apical bias is cell-type-specific (RPE) and low severity for a systemically circulating target.

ecd_size_assessment.rationale: ECD length 1641 residues (>=200) -> large; computed deterministically from DeepTMHMM topology.

epitope_masking.rationale: C3 undergoes major conformational changes upon hydrolysis/activation: neoepitopes in the C3(D) cluster are conformation-dependent and only accessible after activation (a1_evi_17, a1_evi_18). In the convertase complex, substrate C3 binds C4b through two interfaces with conformational rearrangement (a1_evi_33), and the TED/C3d domain is partially occluded until activation (a1_evi_43). Soft-corona/ECM proteins can shield C3 fragment epitopes from receptor access (a1_evi_28, a1_evi_29). No homo-oligomer-driven masking is supported by the ledger. The deterministic prior is_homo_oligomer=false is consistent.

evidence.claim: C3 belongs to the AMCOM thioester protein family (C3, C4, C5, CD109, α2-macroglobulin), establishing its structural classification as a secreted complement glycoprotein without a transmembrane domain.

spans.quote: The vertebrate AMCOM family of thioester proteins includes complement components C3, C4, C5, CD109, and α2-macroglobulin ( Solomon et al., 2004 ).

evidence.claim: C3 contains the conserved thioester linkage consensus sequence GCGEQ and a furin cleavage site — the structural basis for covalent surface attachment via thioester transacylation and proteolytic activation into C3a/C3b.

spans.quote: Each family member has several conserved sequence motifs, including a furin cleavage site and, with the exception of C5, the thioester linkage consensus sequence GCGEQ that is capable of forming a covalent bond with substrates through thioester transacylation.

evidence.claim: C3 is a 185 kDa secreted glycoprotein that is the most abundant complement component in circulation; it has no transmembrane domain and is present both extracellularly and intracellularly in hepatocytes.

spans.quote: Among the nine major components of CS [C1-C9], complement C3, a 185 kDa glycoprotein, is most abundant both in circulation and intracellularly and is central to the complement activation cascades.

evidence.claim: C3b (the large activation fragment of C3) covalently attaches to reactive surfaces via its exposed thioester — this is the primary mechanism by which C3 achieves surface presence; C3 itself is not a transmembrane protein.

spans.quote: This initial convertase complex cleaves native C3 into C3a and C3b, the latter of which covalently attaches to reactive surfaces.

evidence.claim: Surface-bound C3b forms the AP proconvertase complex (C3bB) on cell/pathogen surfaces, confirming that the C3b isoform is functionally accessible on membrane surfaces.

spans.quote: Surface-bound C3b then binds FB, forming the AP C3 proconvertase complex (i.e., C3bB) that is also activated by FD to generate the fully-active AP C3 convertase (i.e., C3bBb).

evidence.claim: Flow cytometry (live-cell, single-cell gated to exclude Candida and dead cells) detected intracellular C3 protein and its cleavage fragments C3a and C3b within vaginal epithelial cells (VECs), characterizing a complosome-type C3 pool in a non-hepatic cell type.

spans.quote: First, we analyzed the presence of C3 and its cleavage fractions C3a and C3b within VECs.

evidence.claim: Flow cytometry quantification showed ~50% of uninfected VECs are C3-positive, rising to ~63% after Candida infection, establishing a baseline and infection-induced increase in C3 protein presence in these epithelial cells.

spans.quote: Our results show that approximately half of the VECs analyzed were positive for C3 (49.8%), with a non-significant increase after infection with either the Colonizing (63.2%) or VVC (63.8%) strain ( Figure 1 a).

evidence.claim: Flow cytometry showed C3b-positive VECs at 8.5% at baseline rising to 20–30% post-Candida infection, indicating activation-dependent generation and retention of C3b fragment within/on VECs.

spans.quote: Similarly, the fraction of C3b-positive VECs was low in uninfected cells (8.5%) and significantly increased after infection with both the Colonizing (29.9%) and VVC (20.3%) strains.

evidence.claim: Anti-human C3b/iC3b/C3d mouse monoclonal antibody clone 1H8 (Hycult Biotech) was used for flow cytometric detection of C3 fragments in VECs; no RRID provided but vendor and clone are specified.

spans.quote: The monoclonal antibodies mouse anti-human complement component-5 (C5) (clone 10B6), mouse anti-human C5a/C5a des-arg (clone 2042) and mouse anti-human C3b/iC3b/C3d (clone 1H8) were kindly provided by Hycult Biotech (Uden, The Netherlands).

evidence.claim: Flow cytometry gating strategy on live VECs was designed to exclude Candida particles and dead/doublet cells, improving specificity of C3 surface/intracellular measurement.

spans.quote: The gating strategy was set to analyze only VECs, removing interference from C. albicans and considering only living single cells ( Figure S1 ).

evidence.claim: Flow synaptometry on synaptosome preparations quantified the fraction of C3b-labeled excitatory (VGLUT1+) synaptosomes in mouse cerebellum, directly measuring C3b surface deposition on synaptic membranes; validated reduction with aging.

spans.quote: Using flow synaptometry to quantify the composition of synapses in more detail, we validated the reduction of C3b-labeled excitatory synaptosomes while the overall frequency of glutamatergic synaptosomes remained unaffected by aging.

evidence.claim: Proteoglycans brevican and aggrecan (neural extracellular matrix components) were upregulated in middle-aged cerebellum, and ECM-mediated shielding of synapses is proposed to reduce complement C3-tagging — a form of epitope masking that limits C3b accessibility at the synapse surface.

spans.quote:
Notably, proteoglycans brevican and aggrecan, key components of the neural extracellular matrix, were significantly upregulated in the middle-aged CB.

Discussion

The data presented herein suggests the ECM-mediated shielding of synapses from complement-tagging and subsequent engulfment by microglia.

evidence.claim: Native C3, C3b, and C3(H2O) were exposed to activated platelets expressing phospholipid-scrambled membranes (cell-based binding assay), directly testing C3 form-dependent surface accessibility on intact activated cells.

spans.quote: C3, C3b, and C3(H<sub>2</sub>O) were exposed to activated platelets expressing phospholipid-scrambled membranes.

evidence.claim: Native C3 showed the highest binding to activated platelets compared to C3b and soluble C3(H2O), and generated the most efficient C3bBb convertase on the platelet surface — demonstrating that intact C3 preferentially engages phospholipid-scrambled membranes.

spans.quote: Native C3 demonstrated the highest binding to activated platelets compared to C3b and soluble C3(H<sub>2</sub>O) and revealed the most efficient convertase (C3bBb) formation.

evidence.claim: Annexin V competitively inhibited C3 binding to activated platelets, apoptotic PMN, and endothelial cells by blocking phosphatidylserine; properdin enhanced binding and convertase formation — demonstrating that C3 surface accessibility on intact cells is modulated by phospholipid exposure and properdin (a form of membrane-availability gating).

spans.quote: The specific binding of annexin V to phospholipid-scrambled membranes, inhibited C3 binding to activated platelets, and to apoptotic PMN and endothelial cells, while properdin enhanced both binding and convertase generation.

evidence.claim: In thromboinflammatory disorders, C3(H2O) exists in a bound form on activated platelets and endothelial cells — demonstrating that a soluble, hydrolyzed C3 form can associate with cell surfaces under pathological conditions.

spans.quote: In thromboinflammatory disorders, C3(H<sub>2</sub>O) also exists in a bound form on activated platelets and endothelial cells.

evidence.claim: Anti-C3 monoclonal antibodies 4SD17.3, 7D84.1, 7D169.1, and 7D398.1 (targeting conformational neoepitopes in the C3(D) cluster) were used for sandwich ELISA detection of C3 conformational states; produced per Nilsson et al.

spans.quote: The anti-C3 monoclonal antibodies (mAb) 4SD17.3, 7D84.1, 7D169.1, and 7D398.1 against different neoepitopes in C3 (the C3(D) cluster) were produced according to Nilsson et al.

evidence.claim: Three additional mAb clones (7D169.1, 7D84.1, 7D398.1) all confirmed the conformational change in C3 upon hydrolysis — orthogonal antibody validation strategy confirming C3 epitope accessibility is conformation-dependent.

spans.quote: The fractions were also tested with three other mAbs against conformational epitopes (clones 7D169.1, 7D84.1, 7D398.1), which all confirmed the conformational change in the molecule.

evidence.claim: Mature (HM) iPSC-RPE cells secreted increased C3a, C3(H2O), and iC3b apically and basally, while less mature (LM) cells retained C3 fragments intracellularly — demonstrating maturation-dependent partitioning between secreted/shed C3 forms and intracellular retention. This is evidence that the dominant C3 species in RPE is secreted rather than membrane-anchored.

spans.quote: HM iPSC-RPE secreted increased levels of C3a, C3(H2O), and iC3b, whereas LM cells retained C3 fragments intracellularly.

evidence.claim: Western blotting and immunostaining of iPSC-RPE showed apical localization of C3 protein and intracellular C3 β-chain — indicating that in epithelial cells C3 is present at apical membrane/secretory surface and intracellularly, not as a stable basolateral PM protein.

spans.quote:
Western blotting and immunostaining revealed maturation-dependent C3 fragment profiles, with apical localization of C3 and intracellular presence of the intact C3 β chain in HM iPSC-RPE, while C3 fragments were present in LM and ARPE-19 cells.

Conclusions

HM iPSC-RPE cells most closely mimic native RPE features and provide a robust model for in vitro studies.

evidence.claim: Intracellular complement C3 processing in iPSC-RPE was characterized using epitope-specific antibodies — methodological detail confirming antibody-based assay for C3 in non-hepatic cells; permeabilization required for intracellular detection.

spans.quote:
Intracellular complement C3 processing was characterized using epitope-specific antibodies.

Results

HM conditions enhanced epithelial features in both models, with HM iPSC-RPE displaying enhanced apical localization of RPE markers, polarity, reduced cilia length, and higher transepithelial resistance.

evidence.claim: Immunohistochemistry on post-mortem human tauopathy brain tissue detected C1q and complement activation markers iC3b and TCC on neurons, with C3b/iC3b deposition particularly prominent on neuronal surfaces — direct IHC evidence of C3 fragment deposition on a human cell surface.

spans.quote: C1q and the complement activation markers iC3b and terminal complement complex (TCC) were assessed by immunohistochemistry and were elevated in all tauopathy cases compared to controls, with C1q and C3b/iC3b deposition particularly prominent on neurons, demonstrating complement activation on these cells.

evidence.claim: Surface deposition of C3 (along with MAC) was measured on infected cells and found to be greatly reduced on persistently infected (PI) vs acutely infected (AI) cells — direct surface deposition evidence using an implied flow or IF surface assay without permeabilization.

spans.quote: Surface deposition of membrane attack complex (MAC) and C3 was also greatly reduced on the surface of PI cells compared to AI cells.

evidence.claim: Flow cytometry on serum-treated bacteria confirmed that only iC3b (not C3b) was present on their surface due to rapid C3b cleavage — direct evidence that iC3b is the dominant surface-deposited C3 fragment on serum-resistant bacterial isolates.

spans.quote: They solely activated the alternative pathway (AP), and only iC3b was present on their surface due to a rapid cleavage of C3b.

evidence.claim: Anti-rat C3/C3b mouse monoclonal antibody clone 2B10B9B2 (Hycult Biotech, cat HM3031) used in complement-assay studies — antibody identifier pairing with C3 detection methods (allows downstream annotation of antibody validation).

spans.quote: Antibodies for detection of rat DAF (clone RDIII-7 cat no: HM3035), rat C3/C3b (clone 2B10B9B2, cat no: HM3031), rat Crry (clone TLD-1C11, cat no: HM3032) and rat C5aR (clone mAb R63, cat no: HM3017) were purchased from Hycult (Uden, the Netherlands).

evidence.claim: IHC demonstrated positive C3 staining in protein casts and within proximal tubule epithelial cells in nephrotic mouse kidney — tissue-level localization evidence showing C3 protein in epithelial compartment; surface vs intracellular not distinguished by IHC alone.

spans.quote: Immunohistochemistry demonstrated positive C3 staining in protein casts and within the proximal tubule.

evidence.claim: C3-knockout mice (C3−/−) were used in a nephrotic mouse model, providing a genetic loss-of-function control validating C3 specificity of complement-pathway readouts.

spans.quote: These mice were intercrossed with mice deficient for complement factor B ( Cfb −/− [ 20 ]),, complement factor D ( Cfd −/− [ 21 ]), and complement factor C3 ( C3 −/− [ 22 ]), to yield Nphs2 Δipod *Cfb −/− , Nphs2 Δipod *Cfd −/− or Nphs2 Δipod *C3 −/− mice, respectively.

evidence.claim: HC-bound iC3b on nanoparticles drives macrophage uptake via complement receptors; soft-corona proteins attenuate this by shielding/masking C3 fragment accessibility — direct functional evidence that soft-corona proteins act as epitope-masking agents limiting iC3b accessibility to complement receptors.

spans.quote: Functional assays demonstrate that macrophage uptake is primarily driven by HC-bound iC3b, while SC proteins attenuate this process by shielding HC-bound C3 fragments and limiting their accessibility to complement receptors.

evidence.claim: SC (soft-corona) proteins mask HC-bound C3 fragments on nanoparticle surfaces, reducing their accessibility to complement receptors on macrophages — mechanistic evidence for epitope masking of C3/iC3b by protein corona analogous to ECM shielding in biological contexts.

spans.quote: Importantly, the macrophage uptake of NPs was primarily governed by HC‐bound C3 fragments, whereas SC proteins may attenuate uptake by masking C3 fragments associated with the HC, thereby reducing their accessibility to CRs.

evidence.claim: A cocrystal structure of compstatin analogue Cp01 in complex with C3c fragment is available and was used to guide SAR study — crystal structure evidence for a small-molecule inhibitor engaging the surface-accessible C3c epitope.

spans.quote: Finally, an experimentally achieved cocrystal structure of Cp01 in complex with the target fragment C3c is available, which has been used to guide the SAR study ( Figure A).

evidence.claim: Cryo-EM structure of C3bB-Cp60 complex at 2.88 Å resolution confirms the structural basis for compstatin (Cp60) binding to C3b — direct structural evidence of therapeutic engagement of the C3 ECD by a clinical-stage compstatin inhibitor.

spans.quote: Cryogenic electron microscopy of the C3bB-Cp60 complex at 2.88 Å resolution confirmed the structural basis for enhanced target affinity and provided mechanistic insights.

evidence.claim: Compstatins (including clinical-stage analogs) directly inhibit C3 activation by binding the C3 ECD — therapeutic engagement of soluble/deposited C3 as surface accessibility evidence; these binders engage C3/C3b at the convertase-forming surface.

spans.quote: Compstatins directly impair the activation of C3, a central hub of the complement cascade, which can provide advantages over other clinical inhibitors.

evidence.claim: Structural study revealed that substrate C3 binds C4b of the CP convertase through two interfaces (one shared with the SCIN-inhibited C3bBb dimer), defined by conformational changes in C3 — direct structural evidence of C3 ECD accessibility in convertase complex.

spans.quote: Substrate C3 binds C4b of the convertase through two interfaces: one also found in the SCIN-inhibited C3bBb dimer, and another facilitated by conformational changes in C3.

evidence.claim: Anti-C3 nanobody hC3Nb3 was used in a therapeutic duo-nanobody construct targeting C3/C4b — names the nanobody binder engaging C3 ECD; validates therapeutic accessibility of C3 epitope.

spans.quote: A duo-nanobody construct was engineered by fusing anti-C4b 38 and anti-C3 nanobody hC3Nb3 40 separated by a linker containing a non-complement specific nanobody 4C8 48 flanked by thrombin cleavage sites followed by 3xGSS repeats.

evidence.claim: C3 has documented intracellular functions in autophagy, ER function, and lipid homeostasis in hepatocytes — evidence that a significant pool of C3 resides intracellularly in its major producing cell type, consistent with the secreted/intracellular rather than membrane-anchored classification.

spans.quote: Beyond its extracellular functions, intracellular C3 plays important roles in autophagy, endoplasmic reticulum function, and lipid homeostasis in the hepatocytes [ 6 ].

evidence.claim: Flow cytometry was used to measure intracellular C3 levels in immune cells of HBV-infected patients — intracellular C3 detection in immune cells by flow cytometry (implies permeabilization); non-surface measurement qualifying the surface-accessibility read.

spans.quote: Flow cytometry was used to determine the intracellular C3 levels in immune cells of HBV-infected patients and the influence of viral and host factors on C3 expression.

evidence.claim: C3 mRNA was profoundly decreased in liver biopsy tissue of chronic hepatitis B patients vs uninfected individuals — RNA-level expression evidence; confirms hepatic C3 transcription but provides no surface-accessibility information.

spans.quote: In parallel, we observed a profound decrease in C3 mRNA expression in liver biopsy tissues of CHB patients than uninfected individuals (Fig. 1 B).

evidence.claim: C3 and CFH were localized on human photoreceptors by immunostaining — direct evidence of C3 protein presence at photoreceptor cell surfaces in human retina, supporting a non-canonical extracellular/surface-accessible C3 pool in a specialized neuronal cell type.

spans.quote: In addition to revealing a novel role for an AMCOM family member in sensory neuron structure and protein transport, the localization of C3 and CFH on human photoreceptors provides support for noncanonical models of AMD and EODM pathogenesis in which defects in cilia structure and protein transport contribute directly to the progressive photoreceptor dysfunction that characterizes these diseases.

evidence.claim: Complement activation on a cancer cell surface was evidenced by deposition of C3 (the central complement cascade player) detected by immunostaining on Panc02 murine pancreatic cancer cells after complement-activating treatment.

spans.quote: S1B) and activation of complement on the cell surface, as evidenced by the deposition of C3, the central player in the complement cascade (Supplementary Fig.

evidence.claim: In vitro binding assay showed dose-dependent binding of the therapeutic fusion protein FHR51-9FH1-5 to surface-immobilized C3, C3b, iC3b, and C3d — direct evidence that these C3 isoforms/fragments are accessible for therapeutic engagement when surface-immobilized.

spans.quote: In vitro FHR51-9FH1-5 showed dose-dependent binding to surface-immobilized C3, C3b, iC3b, and C3d; factor I cofactor activity; and reduced C3a generation in an alternative pathway convertase assay.

evidence.claim: Intracellular complement pathway activation in spleen stromal fibroblastic reticular cells (FRCs) generates C3a via the alternative pathway after SFTSV infection — evidence for intracellular (non-surface) C3 activation in a non-hepatic cell type, reinforcing the intracellular/secreted-rather-than-PM nature of C3.

spans.quote: Infection of spleen stromal fibroblastic reticular cells with SFTSV significantly induces intracellular complement pathways through a type I IFN-independent manner and generates the active component C3a through the alternative pathway.

evidence.claim: CR2-fH (a therapeutic complement inhibitor) targets C3b deposited on membranes via its CR2 domain, and was shown to significantly reduce C3b deposition and complement activation on RPE cell membranes — therapeutic engagement of surface-deposited C3b as surface accessibility evidence.

spans.quote: Overall, CR2 targeting of the inhibitory domain of fH is more effective than SCR-mediated binding of the endogenous full-length fH in blocking deposition of C3b and ensuing complement activation on membranes. 22 , 39 Here, we showed that CR2-fH significantly reduced or prevented CC-HS–induced TEER reduction, complement activation (MAC), APOE deposition, lipid accumulation (Nile Red), and autophagy impairment in RPE cells.

evidence.claim: The C-terminal domain of staphylococcal Efb (Efb-C) forms a high-affinity interaction with the thioester-containing domain (TED/C3d) of C3b, blocking C3 proconvertase formation — direct structural evidence of C3b TED accessibility as a therapeutic target epitope.

spans.quote: The C-terminal domain of Efb (Efb-C) forms a high-affinity interaction with the thioester-containing domain of C3b (TED/C3d), thereby blocking formation of the C3 proconvertase complex through an allosteric mechanism.

evidence.claim: Western blot detection of C3 and C5 protein in whole placental tissue extracts from PE and GDM pregnancies — whole-tissue Western blot without fractionation; confirms C3 protein expression in placenta but does not distinguish surface from intracellular localization.

spans.quote:
The expressions of two additional complement components, C3 and C5, were also detected by western blot.

Results

The placentae from PE and GDM showed substantial collagen and fibrinoid deposition, thicker foetal blood capillaries, and a considerable number of syncytial knots.

evidence.claim: C3 is primarily produced by hepatocytes of the liver as the main source of serum-circulating complement C3; additionally, C3 is expressed by various immune and non-immune cell populations where it functions locally or intracellularly (complosome).

spans.quote: While the main source of serum-circulating complement components is the hepatocytes of the liver, some complement proteins are also expressed by various immune and non-immune cell populations and these locally or intracellularly acting complement components (complosome) are critical in maintaining cellular homeostasis and effector responses [ 4 , 5 ].

evidence.claim: C3 mRNA expression is profoundly decreased in liver biopsy tissues of chronic hepatitis B (CHB) patients compared with uninfected individuals, indicating disease-state suppression of hepatic C3 production.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: liver biopsy tissue (chronic hepatitis B patients vs uninfected)

spans.quote: In parallel, we observed a profound decrease in C3 mRNA expression in liver biopsy tissues of CHB patients than uninfected individuals (Fig. 1 B).

evidence.claim: Beyond its extracellular secreted functions, intracellular C3 plays important roles in autophagy, endoplasmic reticulum function, and lipid homeostasis within hepatocytes, indicating an intracellular (non-surface) compartment for C3 in liver cells.

spans.quote: Beyond its extracellular functions, intracellular C3 plays important roles in autophagy, endoplasmic reticulum function, and lipid homeostasis in the hepatocytes [ 6 ].

evidence.claim: C3 is produced by extrahepatic immune cells including T cells and monocytes/macrophages, where it acts as a regulator of immune responses (complosome); this establishes T cells and monocytes/macrophages as non-hepatic C3-expressing cell types.

spans.quote: The extrahepatic production of C3 has been observed in different immune-cells including T cells, and monocyte/macrophages, where this complement-component acts as regulators of immune response [ 4 ].

evidence.claim: Complement proteins including C3 are expressed in immune cells (monocytes, macrophages, T lymphocytes) and non-immune cells (epithelial cells, fibroblasts, astrocytes), in addition to the liver-derived soluble circulating pool.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: monocytes, macrophages, T lymphocytes, epithelial cells, fibroblasts, astrocytes

cell_context.cell_type: monocytes, macrophages, T lymphocytes, epithelial cells, fibroblasts, astrocytes

spans.quote: Although the soluble complement produced by the liver is a critical part of the protection against pathogens, the complement proteins and receptors are also expressed in immune cells, including monocytes, macrophages, and T-lymphocytes, as well as in non-immune cells, including epithelial cells, fibroblasts, and astrocytes [ 27 ].

evidence.claim: In SFTSV-infected IFNAR-/- mice, complement hyperactivation occurs specifically in the spleen, and the spleen serves as a source of C3; this establishes splenic tissue as a site of C3 production under viral infection (disease-state-induced).

spans.quote: In this study, by utilizing a lethal mouse model of IFNAR-/- mice, we discovered that complement hyperactivation occurs in the spleen, and the spleen serves as a source of the central complement component C3 in SFTSV-infected mice.

evidence.claim: SFTSV infection of splenic fibroblastic reticular cells (FRCs) significantly induces intracellular complement C3 production through a type I IFN-independent manner; C3 is processed via the alternative pathway to generate active C3a intracellularly in these stromal cells.

spans.quote: Infection of spleen stromal fibroblastic reticular cells with SFTSV significantly induces intracellular complement pathways through a type I IFN-independent manner and generates the active component C3a through the alternative pathway.

evidence.claim: Human Müller cells (retinal macroglial cells) secrete components of the complement system upon pro-inflammatory cytokine stimulation, indicating that retinal Müller cells are a source of complement C3 under inflammatory conditions.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: MIO-M1 Müller cell line; primary retinal Müller glia (pRMG)

cell_context.activation_state: cytokine-stimulated (IFNγ, TNFα, IL-4, IL-6, IL-10, VEGF, TGFβ)

spans.quote: Stimulation of Müller cells resulted in a primarily pro-inflammatory phenotype with secretion of cytokines and components of the complement system.

evidence.claim: In retinal pigment epithelium (iPSC-RPE), the complement C3 production and intracellular processing state is maturation-dependent: high-maturation (HM) iPSC-RPE cells secrete increased levels of C3a, C3(H2O), and iC3b, whereas low-maturation (LM) cells retain C3 fragments intracellularly. This demonstrates cell-state-dependent partitioning of C3 between intracellular and secreted/extracellular compartments in RPE.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: iPSC-derived RPE (low maturation vs high maturation)

cell_context.activation_state: low maturation vs high maturation conditions

spans.quote: HM iPSC-RPE secreted increased levels of C3a, C3(H2O), and iC3b, whereas LM cells retained C3 fragments intracellularly.

evidence.claim: In high-maturation iPSC-RPE, C3 shows apical localization by immunostaining, while in low-maturation RPE cells C3 fragments are present intracellularly; C3 is not observed stably at the basolateral plasma membrane surface in either state.

spans.quote:
Western blotting and immunostaining revealed maturation-dependent C3 fragment profiles, with apical localization of C3 and intracellular presence of the intact C3 β chain in HM iPSC-RPE, while C3 fragments were present in LM and ARPE-19 cells.

Conclusions

HM iPSC-RPE cells most closely mimic native RPE features and provide a robust model for in vitro studies.

evidence.claim: C3 protein (intact) and its bioactive fragment C3a are abundantly present in Hedgehog-type medulloblastoma (MB) tumor tissue sections by IHC, whereas neither C3 nor C3a is detectable in tumor-adjacent normal brain tissue — establishing tumor-restricted C3 accumulation in this CNS malignancy.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: medulloblastoma tumor sections vs adjacent normal brain

spans.quote: As shown in Fig. 1 a, tumor sections were obtained from Hh-type MB patients and stained for C3 and C3a proteins, and it was found that both C3 and C3a were abundantly present in the tumor area, whereas they were not detectable in the tumor-adjacent normal tissue area.

evidence.claim: C3 and C3a were not detectable in tumor-adjacent normal brain tissue in medulloblastoma patients, establishing absence of C3 in normal adult cerebellar tissue by IHC — contradicting any assumption of constitutive CNS C3 protein accumulation.

spans.quote: As shown in Fig. 1 a, tumor sections were obtained from Hh-type MB patients and stained for C3 and C3a proteins, and it was found that both C3 and C3a were abundantly present in the tumor area, whereas they were not detectable in the tumor-adjacent normal tissue area.

evidence.claim: C3b/iC3b complement activation markers are elevated in all tauopathy subtypes (Pick's disease, CBD, GGT) compared to controls by IHC; C3b/iC3b deposition is particularly prominent on neurons, demonstrating complement activation and C3 fragment deposition on neuronal cell surfaces in tauopathy brain.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: post-mortem brain — tauopathy (PiD, CBD, GGT) vs controls

spans.quote: C1q and the complement activation markers iC3b and terminal complement complex (TCC) were assessed by immunohistochemistry and were elevated in all tauopathy cases compared to controls, with C1q and C3b/iC3b deposition particularly prominent on neurons, demonstrating complement activation on these cells.

evidence.claim: In mouse models of AD and P301S tauopathy, complement activation (including C3) drives synapse loss; C3 deficiency or inhibition is protective, establishing C3 as a pathologically relevant mediator in neurodegeneration with disease-state-induced elevation.

spans.quote: In mouse models of AD, complement activation drives synapse loss, the main correlate of cognitive decline. 8 Similar findings have emerged from recent studies in P301S tauopathy mice, which show neuronal tau accumulation and involvement of classical and alternative complement pathways in synapse loss, with deficiency or inhibition of C1q or C3 being protective. 9 , 10 , 11 In contrast, others reported that silencing of the complement C3 convertase regulator Crry in P301L tauopathy mice was protective, reducing neuroinflammation, the number of neurons with hyperphosphorylated tau

evidence.claim: COVID-19 patient lung tissues show hyper-activation of complement represented by extensive deposition of C3, C3b/iC3b/C3d, and C5b-9 compared to normal lung tissue, indicating disease-state-induced accumulation of C3 and its cleavage fragments in the alveolar compartment.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: lung paraffin sections (COVID-19 patients vs normal)

spans.quote: The formation of an "alveolar emboli" structure may contribute to thrombosis and consolidation in lung tissue. In addition, we also found that compared to normal tissue, the lung tissues of COVID-19 patients displayed the hyper-activation of complement that is represented by extensive deposition of C3, C3b/iC3b/C3d and C5b-9, and the increased expression level of complement regulatory proteins CD55, and especially CD59 but not CD46. The thrombosis and consolidation in lung tissues may contribute to the pathogenesis of COVID-19.

evidence.claim: C3 glomerulopathy (C3G) is characterized by abnormal glomerular C3 deposition in kidney due to dysregulation of the complement alternative pathway; the glomerulus is the anatomical compartment where C3 deposits accumulate in this kidney disease.

spans.quote: C3 glomerulopathy (C3G) encompasses a group of kidney diseases characterized by abnormal glomerular C3 deposition due to dysregulation of the complement alternative pathway (AP) [ 1 , 2 ].

evidence.claim: C3 staining is detected in injured renal tubules in RIAKI (rhabdomyolysis-associated acute kidney injury) patients, often surrounded by C5aR1-expressing myeloid cells, establishing C3 deposition in tubular epithelium as a disease-state marker in acute kidney injury.

spans.quote: Here we show that complement fragments Ba, Bb, C5a, and sC5b-9 are elevated in the urine of patients with RIAKI, and C3 staining is detected in injured tubules, often surrounded by C5aR1-expressing myeloid cells.

evidence.claim: Complement C3 protein expression is significantly less frequent in lymph node metastases (LNM) of breast cancer compared to matched primary tumors (PT) by IHC, and lower C3 expression in LNM associates with worse prognosis.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: breast cancer primary tumors and lymph node metastases

cell_context.disease_state: luminal breast cancer with lymphatic metastasis

spans.quote: In validation cohort, complement C3 protein was less frequently expressed in LNM than in PT and it was associated with worse prognosis.

evidence.claim: Local complement system expression (including C3) declines during lymphatic spread of non-metastatic luminal breast cancer, with further reduction of tumoral C3 in LNM indicative of poor survival — establishing a disease-state-dependent downregulation of C3 in metastatic breast cancer tissue.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: primary breast tumor and lymph node metastasis tissue

cell_context.tissue: breast cancer (primary tumor vs lymph node metastasis)

spans.quote: To conclude, local expression of the complement system components declines during lymphatic spread of non-metastatic luminal BCa, whilst further reduction of tumoral complement C3 in LNM is indicative for poor survival.

evidence.claim: C3 and C5 levels are elevated in placental tissue of preeclampsia (PE) and recurrent pregnancy loss (RPL) patients compared to normal healthy pregnancy, suggesting overactivation of complement in the placenta in these obstetric disease states.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: placental tissue (PE, GDM, RPL vs normal pregnancy)

spans.quote:
In both PE and RPL placentae, C3 and C5 levels were high, suggesting possibility of overactivation of complement proteins in the placenta.

Discussion

The observed elevated properdin level can contribute to the heightened inflammatory response in PE, GDM and RPL placentae.

evidence.claim: Approximately half of vaginal epithelial cells (VECs) express C3 at baseline (~49.8%), with a non-significant trend toward increase after infection with either colonizing or VVC Candida strains, establishing VECs as a C3-expressing epithelial cell type.

spans.quote: Our results show that approximately half of the VECs analyzed were positive for C3 (49.8%), with a non-significant increase after infection with either the Colonizing (63.2%) or VVC (63.8%) strain ( Figure 1 a).

evidence.claim: C3a-positive VEC fraction is low at baseline (5.4%) but significantly increases after Candida infection (Colonizing: 21.7%; VVC: 27.7%), demonstrating infection-state-induced C3 cleavage within vaginal epithelial cells.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: vaginal epithelial cells (VECs) — uninfected vs Candida-infected

spans.quote: The C3a-positive VEC cell fraction was low in uninfected cells (5.4%), and this fraction significantly increased after infection with both Colonizing (21.7%) and VVC (27.7%) strains.

evidence.claim: Circulating C3a is elevated during aging in both humans and mice; a non-senescent age-associated macrophage subset expanding in visceral adipose tissue is identified as the predominant cellular source of C3a in aging, establishing adipose-resident macrophages as a key extrahepatic C3-producing cell population in the aging context.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: visceral adipose macrophages (age-associated subset); human plasma

spans.quote: Furthermore, circulating C3a is elevated during aging in humans and in mice; we identified a non-senescent age-associated macrophage subset that expands in visceral fat as the predominant source.

evidence.claim: The C3a/C3 ratio in human plasma is significantly lowered by caloric restriction (CR), demonstrating that systemic C3 cleavage/activation state is modulated by nutritional/metabolic intervention, with CR reducing complement-mediated inflammaging.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: plasma (caloric restriction clinical trial, middle-aged adults)

spans.quote: In humans, the C3a/C3 ratio was significantly lowered by CR, thus reducing inflammation emanating from three canonical complement pathways.

evidence.claim: C3(H2O), the hydrolytic product of C3, exists in a cell-bound form on activated platelets and endothelial cells in thromboinflammatory disorders, representing a disease-state-specific surface association of C3 on these host cell types.

spans.quote: In thromboinflammatory disorders, C3(H<sub>2</sub>O) also exists in a bound form on activated platelets and endothelial cells.

evidence.claim: Native C3 demonstrates the highest binding to activated platelets (phospholipid-scrambled membranes) compared to C3b and soluble C3(H2O), and native C3 bound to activated platelets supports the most efficient C3bBb convertase formation — establishing activated platelet surface as a site of preferential C3 engagement.

spans.quote: Native C3 demonstrated the highest binding to activated platelets compared to C3b and soluble C3(H<sub>2</sub>O) and revealed the most efficient convertase (C3bBb) formation.

evidence.claim: C3b-opsonized deposition on platelets could not be detected in E. coli-induced platelet aggregation experiments, indicating that the C3b-dependent platelet aggregation effect is not mediated through direct C3b fragment deposition on the platelet surface.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: human platelets (whole blood E. coli model)

spans.quote: We could not detect opsonized C3b on platelets, indicating that the effect of C3 was not dependent on C3b-fragment deposition on platelets.

evidence.claim: Upon complement activation on target surfaces, C3 is proteolytically activated to C3b, which becomes covalently attached to surface-exposed nucleophiles via transacylation mediated by its reactive internal thioester bond — this is the primary mechanism by which C3b becomes cell-surface associated (opsonization).

spans.quote: Complement activation on target surfaces drives the proteolytic activation of complement factor 3 (C3) to C3b, which becomes covalently attached to surface-exposed nucleophiles via a transacylation mediated by its reactive internal thioester bond.

evidence.claim: C3b staining is augmented in kidney and spleen tissue from HO-1-deficient (Hmox1-/-) rats, demonstrating that reduced complement regulation (DAF/Crry) leads to increased C3b deposition in these tissues under disease conditions.

spans.quote: C3b staining was augmented in the kidney and spleen from Hmox1-/- rats, suggesting that the decrease of DAF and Crry was sufficient to increase C3b deposition.

evidence.claim: In human skin tissue, mast cells express C3 (detected as C3c immunoreactivity) in healthy skin (34.2%), initial petechial (37.4%), and palpable purpura (43.4%) vasculitis lesions, establishing mast cells as a C3-producing cell type in skin with a modest increase in disease states.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: skin mast cells (healthy skin vs vasculitis lesions)

spans.quote: The effect of rh-chymase on purified human C3, C3a and IgG was studied using SDS-PAGE electrophoresis and LAD2 mast cell cultures. The results show that 34.2 ± 17.9, 37.4 ± 15.5 and 43.4 ± 18.6 % (mean ± SD) of the mast cells express C3c immunoreactivity in the healthy skin, initial petechial (IP) and palpable purpura (PP) lesions, respectively. About 9.4-12.1 % of the chymase(+) mast cells were in apparent contact with C3c(+) vessels in IP and PP. The treatment of cryosections with rh-chymase decreased the IF staining of C3c, but not that of immunoglobulins.

evidence.claim: In IL-33- and TNF-α-stimulated eosinophils (multi-omics analysis), C3 is upregulated as an inflammatory mediator, establishing eosinophils as a C3-expressing cell type under cytokine-activated (inflammatory) conditions.

spans.quote:
Multi-omics analyses, including transcriptomics and proteomics, were performed. Extracellular trap formation (ETosis) was assessed by SYTOX nucleic acid staining and was visualised by immunofluorescence and transmission electron microscopy.

Results

Multi-omics analyses revealed an IL-33- and TNF-α-induced inflammatory gene signature characterised by the upregulation of cell surface markers (oxLDL receptor 1, CD22, CD4 and ICAM-1) and inflammatory mediators (C3, CCL3/4 and IL1A/B). CD22 upregulation was specific to IL-33 stimulation.

standing: surface-reachability barriers (BBB / tumor penetration / luminal / accessibility)
standing: co-receptor / partner-dependency for surface trafficking
standing: state-conditional surface biology (activation / stress / disease / EMT / senescence / differentiation) — feeds accessibility_modulation
standing: normal-tissue surface expression (six high-consequence tox organs)

confidence_reasoning: Confidence is low because C3 is canonically a secreted plasma protein with no transmembrane domain, and all surface-accessibility evidence reflects state-conditional covalent deposition of activated fragments (C3b/iC3b) rather than stable plasma-membrane anchoring of the intact protein. The direct surface evidence is limited to a single live-cell flow cytometry report in virus-infected cells (one source, infection-state dependent) and a functional inhibitor assay on RPE cells; no independent corroboration exists with genetic controls or orthogonal surface proteomics. The dominant high-severity secreted-form risk means any circulating antibody must compete with a vast excess of soluble C3 in plasma (~1 mg/mL). Lifting confidence would require independent live-cell surface proteomics (e.g., cell-surface biotinylation with knockout validation) confirming C3 fragment deposition on a defined target-cell population, and quantification of the surface-to-soluble ratio under the proposed targeting condition.
